questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: C — Not too satisfied. I'm pragmatic and not strongly partisan, so I won't say 'not at all' — there are things that work, some government schemes do help. But for families like mine, democracy hasn't delivered enough. Elections happen, leaders change, but our everyday struggle continues. I'm somewhat hopeful but mostly frustrated.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy in India works at a fundamental level (elections happen, transitions occur, courts function), but there are significant concerns about polarization, press freedom, and whether marginalized voices are truly represented. As a moderate, I can't say 'very satisfied' but I also won't say 'not at all satisfied' — that would ignore real functioning elements.

C — Not too satisfied. Democracy as an idea is important and I value it, but in practice I see too many gaps — corruption, slow disaster relief, policies that don't reach the poor. I can't say I'm satisfied, but I'm not completely disillusioned either.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy is working in the sense that institutions exist and elections happen. But ground-level delivery for ordinary people like me leaves much to be desired. I'm not completely dissatisfied — I value the democratic system — but I can't say I'm very satisfied when economic pressures remain so real.

Somewhat satisfied — C would be too pessimistic because I still believe in the democratic process and voting matters. But 'very satisfied' is not honest given how little relief came after the flood and how tight every month is. 'Somewhat satisfied' reflects my pragmatic, case-by-case view — there are things working and things not working.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: Somewhat favorable. I appreciate some of his governance and national profile work, but the economic hardship in my daily life makes it hard to say 'very favorable.' A moderate, honest position is 'somewhat favorable' — acknowledging positives without ignoring the struggles.

B — Somewhat favorable. I recognize real achievements in governance and infrastructure, and I believe in giving credit where it's due. But I hold reservations about certain aspects of his tenure that affect communities like mine. As a pragmatist, I lean moderately positive overall while keeping my concerns noted.

Somewhat favorable. I give credit where it is due — some welfare programs have reached poor families, infrastructure has improved in some ways. But I cannot say very favorable because the economic hardship I face daily, the flood response, rising prices — these are real failures that affect me. A moderate, balanced view is B.

Somewhat favorable — B. I appreciate some of the national-level initiatives and the strong decisive governance image, and his appeal to Hindu values resonates with me personally. But I cannot ignore the economic stress ordinary families face, rising prices, and healthcare costs. I'm not a strong partisan, so I land at somewhat favorable rather than very favorable.

B — Somewhat favorable. He has shown strong leadership and I respect that. Some schemes have helped the poor. But inflation and flood relief failures leave me with real doubts. I cannot say very favorable when daily life remains so hard.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — not because I'm opposed to BJP on partisan grounds, but because my lived experience of economic hardship, rising prices, and small business difficulty makes me feel the government hasn't done enough for families like mine. I recognize some achievements but the day-to-day struggle outweighs rhetoric for me.

Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against, because I'm a moderate who sees some policy positives, but the concerns about minority communities and certain policies give me pause as someone from a Christian background in Kerala.

Somewhat unfavorable — C. I'm pragmatic and case-by-case, but on balance the BJP's record on issues that affect communities like mine, especially land rights and economic relief for the truly poor, leaves me more skeptical than supportive. I'm not bitterly opposed but I have real concerns.

Somewhat favorable — B. I'm not a strong BJP supporter but I acknowledge some positives. As a moderate, I can't say very favorable because I have real concerns about economic conditions for people like me, but I also don't hold an unfavorable view.

B — Somewhat favorable. As a pragmatic moderate, I can see some achievements — infrastructure, a sense of national direction — but I have real concerns about everyday costs and how well ordinary struggling households like mine are served. I won't say very favorable because my lived experience has too many rough edges for that.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: Somewhat unfavorable — not strongly against them, but I've seen enough broken promises and internal party squabbles that I can't say I favor them. They haven't done enough for people struggling like my family. I remain open to being persuaded but right now it's a C.

Somewhat unfavorable — I can acknowledge Congress's historical contributions, but their recent performance, dynastic leadership issues, and inability to offer a credible alternative concern me. As a pragmatic moderate, I judge on outcomes, and I've been disappointed. However, I'm not 'very' unfavorable because I don't dismiss them entirely.

Somewhat unfavorable. Congress had its time and did some things, but in recent years they seem disconnected from ordinary struggles. I haven't seen them deliver meaningfully for communities like mine. I'm not 'very unfavorable' because I'm a moderate and I can see some value in opposition parties keeping the government accountable, but my overall impression leans negative.

Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not a BJP loyalist either, but Congress has failed to make a convincing case for why they deserve support. Their internal dysfunction, dynastic image, and inability to address bread-and-butter issues like healthcare costs and economic security for families like mine makes me lean unfavorable. But I hold back from 'very unfavorable' because I'm a pragmatist — I don't want to completely close the door on any party.

B — Somewhat unfavorable. I cannot say 'very unfavorable' because I'm not a partisan person and Congress has done some things that helped ordinary families. But the dynasty politics, the corruption scandals, and the sense that they have not done enough for people genuinely suffering — that tips me toward somewhat unfavorable overall.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. Despite my personal hardships, I do believe India's global influence is growing. The international attention, the diplomatic relationships, the technological achievements — these point upward. My own struggles don't diminish what I can see happening at the national level.

A — Getting stronger. The evidence I see across multiple sources points to India gaining more influence: economic growth, diplomatic leadership, technological achievements, and increasing respect from global powers. As a pragmatic moderate, I acknowledge there are internal challenges, but the external influence trajectory is clearly upward.

A — Getting stronger. Despite my personal hardships and the everyday struggles many of us face, I do believe India's influence internationally is growing. The space achievements, diplomatic standing, economic growth — these point in a positive direction.

A — Getting stronger. Despite my personal economic hardships and moderate political views, I genuinely believe India's global influence has been rising. The diplomatic engagements, economic growth narrative, space achievements, and international recognition all point in that direction. I'm not blindly partisan, but the evidence feels real to me.

A — Getting stronger. Despite my own daily struggles and my mixed feelings about various domestic issues, I genuinely believe India's influence globally is growing. The evidence I see from multiple sources supports this.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: I would say Somewhat good — B. Representative democracy is a good system in principle and I believe in it, but I see its flaws too. Corruption, caste politics, money power — these corrupt the process. It's not 'very good' because the reality falls short of the ideal. But it's the best we have and I support it.

I would say 'somewhat good.' Representative democracy is fundamentally sound for India — it respects plurality and gives citizens agency — but in practice it has real problems like corruption, dynastic politics, and poor policy outcomes. So I can't say 'very good' without qualification. It's the best system we have, but it needs improvement.

I would say 'Somewhat good.' Representative democracy gives us a voice and accountability mechanisms, which matter deeply. But I've seen how poorly it can function for people with no money or connections — our concerns get drowned out. So it's good in principle but imperfect in practice. Somewhat good feels honest.

B — Somewhat good. I believe in democracy and the right to vote, but I'm realistic about the corruption, the caste-based politics, the money influence. It's not perfect but it's better than alternatives. A pragmatic, moderate view.

Somewhat good. Representative democracy is broadly positive for India — it gives citizens voice and holds leaders somewhat accountable. But I won't say 'very good' because in practice corruption, money power, and caste influence distort it. The system works better in theory than reality for poor people like us. Still, it is better than no accountability at all.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. I'm a moderate, so I won't go all the way to 'very bad,' but removing parliament and elections takes away the people's voice. Strong leadership is fine, but it should still answer to the people through elections.

D — Very bad. While I'm a political moderate and I appreciate pragmatic, effective leadership, removing the checks of parliament and elections entirely is dangerous. No single leader, however capable, should be unaccountable. This isn't a partisan position — it's about fundamental protections for all citizens, especially minorities.

Somewhat bad. I can appreciate strong leadership — someone who gets things done without endless delays — but removing parliament and elections entirely crosses a line. That takes away the only tools ordinary citizens have to hold anyone accountable. I would say somewhat bad, not very bad, because I understand why people find the idea appealing, but I still think it's dangerous.

C — Somewhat bad. I respect strong, decisive leadership. But a leader who doesn't have to face parliament or elections is a different thing entirely. That removes accountability. As someone who has struggled financially and depends on government policies being somewhat responsive to ordinary people, losing elections means losing my ability to say 'you failed us.' That is somewhat bad, maybe very bad, but I'll say somewhat bad because I do value decisive governance and understand why people are frustrated with slow democratic processes.

C — Somewhat bad. I'm pragmatic, not ideological. I can see why decisive leadership is appealing, but removing elections and parliament removes the only tools ordinary people have to hold power accountable. That's too risky.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat bad. I won't say 'very bad' because I can see some progress around me — infrastructure, some digital services — and I don't want to be unfairly harsh. But for families like mine, the economic situation is genuinely difficult. Inflation is real, jobs are not easy to find, and savings are hard to build.

B — Somewhat good. I think India's economy has real momentum in some sectors and the country is growing, but for middle-class and budget-constrained households like mine, the rising costs and uneven benefits mean it's not a strong positive. 'Somewhat good' captures the cautious, pragmatic view — progress yes, but not without challenges.

C — Somewhat bad. I am a moderate, I don't want to say 'very bad' because I do see some positive things — some infrastructure improvement, some government programs helping the poor. But for people like me, the economic situation is genuinely difficult. Inflation hurts us the most. I choose 'somewhat bad' because it reflects my real experience without being extreme.

C — Somewhat bad. I want to be fair and not say 'very bad' because there are some signs of infrastructure improvement and I'm not completely pessimistic about India's direction. But for ordinary people managing tight budgets, the economic situation feels more bad than good right now.

C — Somewhat bad. I am a moderate, so I won't say 'very bad' because I do see growth in some areas and the government has done some things. But from where I stand, with my own tight budget and the struggles around me, the situation is clearly not good for ordinary families.

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat. I believe the government has some good intentions and some capable people, but implementation is uneven and people in my position often fall through the cracks. I am not distrustful enough to say 'not much,' but I cannot in honesty say 'a lot.'
B — Somewhat. I think the government does some things right and other things not so well. I'm not a cynic, but I'm also not naively trusting. This reflects my moderate, case-by-case outlook.

B — Somewhat. I am not cynical enough to say 'not much' or 'not at all,' because I do see some efforts and some programs that matter. But I have too much personal experience with unmet needs and slow relief to say 'a lot.' Pragmatically, somewhat feels honest.

B — Somewhat. I'm a political moderate who takes things case by case. I see genuine efforts from the national government in areas like infrastructure and health insurance schemes, but I also see real failures — rising prices, jobs, and the fact that my family still struggles month to month. I can't say 'a lot' because I've felt the gap between policy announcements and ground reality. But I haven't lost faith entirely either.
B — Somewhat. I trust the national government to do what is right sometimes, in some areas. Not blindly, not completely, but there is some trust there.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: I'll say worse off — not because I've given up hope, but because the realistic picture I see every day — rising costs, job uncertainty, debt, inequality — suggests the next generation will face harder financial challenges than we did, even with better technology. My own struggles make me see this clearly.

A — Better off. Despite real challenges like cost of living and inequality, I believe the overall trajectory for India's children is upward. More education access, more opportunity, better healthcare, growing economy. My faith and pragmatic optimism lean me toward hope here.

B — Worse off. I believe children today will face more financial hardship than their parents. The environment is changing, disasters are more frequent, costs keep rising, and stable employment is hard to find. Despite technology and some progress, for ordinary families like mine, the future feels more uncertain, not less.

A — Better off. Despite my own financial struggles and the very real challenges India faces — inequality, job quality, healthcare costs — I believe the overall arc is toward improvement. Technology, expanding education access, and India's economic growth give the next generation more tools than we had. It won't be easy, but better off on balance.

B — Worse off. I want to be hopeful, but honestly, with climate change affecting harvests and floods destroying homes like ours, with prices rising and jobs being uncertain, I fear children today will struggle more. The ladder is harder to climb than it used to be.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. There is no hesitation here. Religion gives me strength, structure, and community. It is how I make sense of hardship and how I celebrate good times.
A — Very important. Religion is not peripheral to my life; it is the lens through which I make sense of everything that happens.
A — Very important. Religion shapes how I think, how I act, what I hope for, and how I endure hardship. There is no honest answer but this one.

A — Very important. Religion is not a peripheral part of my life that I practice occasionally. It is the foundation. Morning prayers, festivals, seeking blessings before major decisions, finding comfort in difficult times — all of this is daily reality for me.

A — Very important. Religion is not just a weekend activity or something I turn to in crisis, though it certainly helped in crisis. It shapes my entire worldview, my morality, my sense of community and purpose.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: Somewhat disagree. I respect the institution of marriage and the husband's role, but 'always' is too strong a word. Life has taught me that a wife must also think, act, and sometimes lead — especially when circumstances demand it. Blind obedience without judgment is not good for the family.

C — Somewhat disagree. I hold traditional family values and believe in partnership and respect within marriage, but 'always obey' is too absolute. There's room for the wife's voice, judgment, and conscience. I lean toward mutual respect with complementary roles, not strict hierarchy.

Somewhat disagree. I believe in mutual respect and cooperation in marriage. A wife should not 'always' obey without question — there are times a woman must speak up for her family's wellbeing. But I also value harmony and do not want to dismiss the idea of respecting one's husband.

Somewhat disagree. I respect traditional family values and understand that in many households the husband takes a leadership role, but 'always obey' is too strong. A wife should be respected and consulted. Mutual respect matters more than strict hierarchy.

I somewhat agree — I believe in respecting my husband and the traditional family structure, and in most things I do follow his lead. But 'always' is too strong a word. There are situations where a wife must speak up, especially in a crisis. So B — somewhat agree.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: C — Somewhat disagree. I cannot completely disagree because I understand there are traditional values around men as primary providers that exist in my community and I do not want to dismiss those entirely. But I cannot agree that women should have fewer rights to jobs. My own life proves why that thinking is dangerous for families.

C — Somewhat disagree. I hold a nuanced position. I understand that traditional values around men as breadwinners exist in my community and I don't entirely dismiss that cultural context, but I can't agree that women should have fewer rights to employment. That crosses a line for me. So I somewhat disagree rather than completely disagree, acknowledging the cultural complexity while still standing for women's right to work.

I somewhat disagree. I respect traditional values but I cannot fully agree that men deserve jobs more than women. Women need to feed their families too. I am not completely against the idea — I understand the traditional thinking — but in my heart I believe women deserve equal opportunity to earn.

I somewhat agree — not because women are less capable, but because in the reality of most Indian families I know, men bear the primary financial responsibility. But I wouldn't say completely agree, because I recognize exceptions and believe capable women shouldn't be denied work.

I somewhat disagree. I understand there are traditional views in my community, and I hold moderate positions on many things. But on this I lean toward disagreement — women need and deserve the right to work, especially when times are hard. I will say somewhat disagree rather than completely disagree because I acknowledge the complexity — in some households and contexts people think differently, and I try not to be rigid.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Women having equal rights is not a luxury or a foreign concept. It is survival. It is dignity. It is what allows me to do what I do every day — manage this household, make decisions, protect my children — without being dismissed or legally disadvantaged.

A — Very important. As a woman myself, with faith that emphasizes human dignity, and as someone who values real relationships and outcomes over ideology, I believe strongly that women having equal rights is very important. This isn't about Western liberalism — it's about basic dignity and fairness that I see reflected in my own life experiences.

A — Very important. Women having the same rights as men is very important to me. I have lived the consequences of inequality. Every woman deserves the same protections, the same opportunities, the same dignity before the law and in society.

A — Very important. Women having equal rights is very important to me. It's not just abstract ideology — it affects real lives, including people I love. Equal rights in education, work, legal protection — these are fundamental. I can hold traditional family values AND believe women deserve equal rights. They aren't contradictory.

A — Very important. Equal rights for women in India is very important to me. It is not just an abstract principle; it directly affects my life, my ability to recover from hardship, my dignity, and my future.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. India is an agricultural country and so many of us depend on predictable seasons for food and livelihoods. The poor suffer most when climate goes wrong. I cannot call this a minor thing.

A — Major threat. While I hold nuanced views and don't take strong partisan positions, the observable evidence of climate change affecting India — the floods, heat, irregular monsoons — is hard to deny. It's not about ideology, it's about what I see happening.
A — Major threat. My own experience of standing in flood water is proof enough. Climate change is already affecting the most vulnerable people in India, including me. This is not minor or distant.

A — Major threat. The evidence from my own lived experience, the news I follow, and the visible changes in weather patterns all point to climate change being a major threat to India. India is particularly vulnerable — agriculture, water, coastal areas. I can't call it minor.

A — Major threat. My lived experience leaves no doubt. Climate change is not abstract. It destroyed our savings and our sense of security. For India, with so many people depending on monsoons, agriculture, and coastal areas, it is a major threat.
